preamble: The reduction of a complex affricate sound to a simple glottal stop is a dramatic form of lenition known as debuccalization. This occurs when the oral place of articulation is lost, leaving only a laryngeal gesture.

examples.note: Synchronic allophone: /tʃ/ surfaces as [ʔ] in certain metrical positions (non-head syllable codas) due to rhythmic laryngeal alternation.
Partial debuccalization: the stop component [t] of /tʃ/ glottalizes, yielding [ʔʃ] rather than full [ʔ] replacement.
